title: Anal squamous cell carcinoma: HPV-driven
vignette: A 63-year-old man comes to the office due to intermittent rectal bleeding. The bleeding worsens with defecation and is associated with itchiness. Ten years ago, he had a kidney transplant for diabetic nephropathy and reports compliance with posttransplant immunosuppression medications. Vital signs are normal. Physical examination reveals a 2-cm ulcerated mass extending from the anal verge into the rectum. Biopsy shows large, eosinophilic squamous epithelial cells arranged in islands; cells have hyperchromatic, irregular nuclei, and scant cytoplasm. Significant keratinization is present. Which of the following factors is most likely responsible for the development of this patient's anal lesion?

clues.note: Chronic immunosuppression after transplant is a major risk factor for HPV persistence and progression to anogenital squamous cancers. Loss of immune surveillance lets HPV-driven dysplasia advance. This is the key host-risk clue.

clues.text: 2-cm ulcerated mass extending from the anal verge into the rectum
clues.note: An ulcerated/exophytic anal mass with bleeding and pruritus is the classic presentation of anal squamous cell carcinoma. Location at the anal verge points to squamous, HPV-related cancer rather than a glandular rectal adenocarcinoma.

clues.text: large, eosinophilic squamous epithelial cells arranged in islands
clues.note: Islands/nests of large eosinophilic squamous cells with nuclear atypia and KERATINIZATION = squamous cell carcinoma. Anogenital SCC is strongly tied to high-risk HPV (16/18).
Keratin pearls/keratinization confirm a SQUAMOUS lineage. Anal SCC — like cervical, vulvar, penile, and oropharyngeal SCC — is driven by HPV (E6/E7 inactivating p53/Rb).

whatMatters: An immunosuppressed transplant patient with an ulcerated anal-verge mass and biopsy showing keratinizing squamous-cell carcinoma = anal SCC. Its key causal factor is high-risk human papillomavirus (types 16/18) — answer C.
strategy: For anogenital/oropharyngeal squamous cancers, the default etiology is HPV (16/18 via E6/E7 → p53/Rb). Immunosuppression (HIV, transplant), receptive anal intercourse, and smoking amplify risk. Don't pick HHV-8 (Kaposi, vascular) or other viruses that cause non-squamous lesions.

eliminations.label: BRAF mutation — melanoma/colon, not anal SCC
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: BRAF V600E drives melanoma and a subset of colorectal cancers. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: anal SQUAMOUS carcinoma is virally driven (HPV), not a BRAF-mutant tumor; the histology is keratinizing squamous, not melanocytic/glandular. ✅ Correct if: the lesion were a melanoma or BRAF-mutant colon cancer.

eliminations.label: HHV-8 — causes Kaposi (vascular), not squamous (your pick)
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: human herpesvirus 8 causes Kaposi sarcoma (a vascular/spindle-cell tumor in immunosuppressed patients) and some lymphomas. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: the biopsy shows keratinizing SQUAMOUS cells in islands — that's SCC (HPV), not the vascular proliferation of Kaposi. ✅ Correct if: the lesion were violaceous vascular plaques with spindle cells and slit-like vessels.

eliminations.label: mismatch repair mutation — Lynch/colorectal adenocarcinoma
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: MMR defects (MSI) cause Lynch-associated colorectal and endometrial ADENOcarcinomas. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: this is squamous, HPV-driven anal cancer, not a glandular MMR-deficient tumor; histology shows squamous islands, not gland-forming adenocarcinoma. ✅ Correct if: the tumor were a colorectal adenocarcinoma with MSI/Lynch features.

eliminations.label: poxvirus — molluscum, a benign lesion
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: poxvirus (molluscum contagiosum) causes small umbilicated papules. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: molluscum is benign and not carcinogenic; it doesn't produce an invasive keratinizing SCC. ✅ Correct if: the finding were umbilicated papules with Henderson-Patterson (molluscum) bodies.

eliminations.label: Treponema pallidum — syphilis, not malignancy
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: T. pallidum causes syphilis (painless chancre, condyloma lata). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it's an infection, not an oncogenic driver of squamous carcinoma; the biopsy shows invasive SCC, not a spirochetal lesion. ✅ Correct if: the lesion were a painless ulcer (chancre) or moist condyloma lata of secondary syphilis.

reasoning: Immunosuppressed transplant patient with an ulcerated anal-verge mass
Biopsy = keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma (islands of atypical squamous cells)
Anogenital SCC is strongly HPV-associated (16/18, E6/E7)
Causal factor = human papillomavirus → answer C
mechanism: High-risk HPV types 16/18 express E6 and E7 oncoproteins that inactivate p53 and Rb, driving squamous dysplasia and carcinoma at anogenital and oropharyngeal sites. Anal SCC presents with rectal bleeding, pruritus, and an ulcerated/nodular anal mass; histology shows islands of large eosinophilic squamous cells with nuclear atypia and keratin pearls. Immunocompromise (transplant, HIV), receptive anal intercourse, smoking, and female sex increase risk.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Micro/Path > Oncogenic viruses & anogenital cancer: HPV 16/18 (E6→p53, E7→Rb), anal/cervical/vulvar/penile/oropharyngeal SCC, immunosuppression risk. Contrast HHV-8 (Kaposi), EBV, HBV/HCV, HTLV-1.

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: Two steps: histology → SCC (keratinizing squamous islands); site + virus link → anogenital SCC = HPV. Immunosuppression is the amplifier, not the answer.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on squamous cell carcinoma, keratin pearls, E6/E7, p53/Rb, anal verge, Kaposi sarcoma? Tell me which and I'll explain.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: HHV-8 (B, your pick) is the transplant/immunosuppression virus you remember — but it causes Kaposi (vascular), not keratinizing SCC. Let the squamous histology pick HPV.

difficulty.label: 🖼️ Imaging / diagram
difficulty.message: The histology table contrasts anal SCC (islands of eosinophilic squamous cells, keratin pearls, atypia) with other anogenital lesions. Practice recognizing keratinizing SCC vs vascular Kaposi vs glandular adenocarcinoma.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Missed as B → make a virus-to-tumor card: HPV→squamous (cervix/anus/oropharynx); HHV-8→Kaposi; EBV→nasopharyngeal/Burkitt; HBV/HCV→HCC; HTLV-1→adult T-cell leukemia.

keywords.back: HPV, immunosuppression (HIV/transplant), receptive anal intercourse, smoking, female sex

firstAid.detail: Micro/Path: HPV 16/18 E6/E7, anogenital + oropharyngeal SCC, immunosuppression risk, vaccination.
firstAid.location: Micro/Path: HPV 16/18 E6/E7, anogenital + oropharyngeal SCC, immunosuppression risk, vaccination.

firstAid.detail: Path: HHV-8 (Kaposi), EBV, HBV/HCV (HCC), HTLV-1; BRAF (melanoma/colon), MMR/MSI (Lynch).
firstAid.location: Path: HHV-8 (Kaposi), EBV, HBV/HCV (HCC), HTLV-1; BRAF (melanoma/colon), MMR/MSI (Lynch).

firstAid.detail: Path: keratin pearls, intercellular bridges, nests/islands of atypical squamous cells.
firstAid.location: Path: keratin pearls, intercellular bridges, nests/islands of atypical squamous cells.

memoryHook: Anogenital/oropharyngeal SQUAMOUS cancer = HPV (16/18). HHV-8 is the VASCULAR (Kaposi) one — don't swap them.
